results.title: [Literature Review] LLM Program Optimization via Retrieval Augmented Search
results.content:
Objective: RAS is designed to iteratively optimize a given program by retrieving relevant examples from a dataset of slow-fast program pairs. Instead of doing an end-to-end optimization, RAS employs a stepwise beam search approach.
    Process:
     + Initialization: RAS starts with a program ( p\_0 ) and aims to find an optimized version ( p' ) which should be semantically equivalent to ( p ).
     + Dynamic Retrieval: At each iteration of the beam search, RAS retrieves the top ( k ) slow-fast program pairs from a training dataset ( \Pi\_{\text{train}} ) based on the semantic similarity of their descriptions generated by an LLM. This contrasts with previous approaches that relied on code similarity. [...] # [Literature Review] LLM Program Optimization via Retrieval Augmented Search

The paper discusses an innovative approach to optimizing programs using large language models (LLMs) and outlines two main methodologies: Retrieval Augmented Search (RAS) and Atomic Edit Guided Search (AEGIS). The researchers aim to enhance the capabilities of LLMs in performing program optimizations, particularly when traditional methods are lacking, and show promising results on benchmark datasets.

### Core Methodology

1. Retrieval Augmented Search (RAS): [...] + Embedding and Similarity: The algorithm uses an embedding function ( \phi ) to convert program descriptions into vector representations, enabling the computation of distances between programs. Specifically, it defines a distance function ( d ) using the L2 norm between these embeddings.
     + Beam Search: RAS conducts ( m ) iterations where, for each current program ( p\_{i-1} ), it samples optimized versions ( p\_j ) using the retrieved examples, and selects the program ( p\_i ) with the best performance based on a defined function ( R ) (e.g., running time). If no improvement is found in performance metrics, it retains the previous version.
2. Atomic Edit Guided Search (AEGIS):

results.title: LLM Program Optimization via Retrieval Augmented Search
results.content:
Abstract: With the advent of large language models (LLMs), there has been great interest in applying them to solve difficult programming tasks. Recent work has demonstrated their potential at program optimization, a key challenge in programming languages research. We propose a blackbox adaptation method called Retrieval Augmented Search (RAS) that performs beam search over candidate optimizations; at each step, it retrieves in-context examples from a given training dataset of slow-fast program pairs to guide the LLM. Critically, we find that performing contextual retrieval based on an LLM-generated natural language description significantly outperforms retrieval based on the source code. In addition, we propose a method called AEGIS for improving interpretability by decomposing training [...] back arrowGo to ICLR 2026 Conference homepage

## LLM Program Optimization via Retrieval Augmented Search

Download PDF

### Sagnik Anupam, Alexander Shypula, Osbert Bastani

19 Sept 2025 (modified: 03 Dec 2025)ICLR 2026 Conference Withdrawn SubmissionEveryoneRevisionsBibTeXCC BY 4.0

Keywords: program optimization, large language models, contextual retrieval

TL;DR: We propose two blackbox adaptation techniques for LLM-guided program optimization using contextual retrieval and beam search. [...] for improving interpretability by decomposing training examples into ''atomic edits'' that are significantly more incremental in nature. We show that RAS performs up to 2.04$\times$ better than prior state-of-the-art blackbox adaptation strategies on optimizing C++ programs, and that AEGIS performs 1.37$\times$ better while performing significantly smaller edits. We also show that using RAS improves the mean runtime percentile of Python programs by 10.27 as compared to other strategies.

results.title: Sagnik Anupam, Alexander Shypula, Osbert Bastani · LLM Program Optimization via Retrieval Augmented Search · SlidesLive
results.content: With the advent of large language models (LLMs), there has been a great deal of interest in applying them to solve difficult programming tasks. Recent work has demonstrated their potential at program optimization, a key challenge in programming languages research. We propose a blackbox adaptation method called Retrieval Augmented Search (RAS) that performs beam search over candidate optimizations; at each step, it retrieves in-context examples from a given training dataset of slow-fast program pairs to guide the LLM. Critically, we find that performing contextual retrieval based on an LLM-generated natural language description significantly outperforms retrieval based on the source code. In addition, we propose a method called AEGIS for improving interpretability by decomposing training [...] for improving interpretability by decomposing training examples into "atomic edits" that are significantly more incremental in nature. We show that RAS performs 1.8 × better than prior state-of-the-art blackbox adaptation strategies, and that AEGIS performs 1.37 × better while performing significantly smaller edits.

results.title: Advanced RAG Techniques: Optimizing Retrieval for LLMs at Scale
results.content:
Retrieval-augmented generation is an important part of LLM applications, but ensuring high-quality, context-aware retrieval remains a challenge, especially when dealing with large, conflicting, or rapidly changing datasets. In this session, we’ll talk about advanced RAG techniques designed to improve retrieval accuracy, personalize chatbot interactions, and reduce hallucinations.
 
You’ll learn how to:
 1. Optimize retrieval quality
 2. Leverage follow-up questions to refine retrieval dynamically based on user intent.
 3. Integrate conversation context to personalize responses and enhance long-term memory.
 4. Handle conflicting data using document scoring, metadata filtering, and confidence-based answer synthesis. [...] salary for developers because it's actually the same. It's very similar. results.title: Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG) for LLMs
results.content:
Copy page

# Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG) for LLMs

There are many challenges when working with LLMs such as domain knowledge gaps, factuality issues, and hallucination. Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG) provides a solution to mitigate some of these issues by augmenting LLMs with external knowledge such as databases. RAG is particularly useful in knowledge-intensive scenarios or domain-specific applications that require knowledge that's continually updating. A key advantage of RAG over other approaches is that the LLM doesn't need to be retrained for task-specific applications. RAG has been popularized recently with its application in conversational agents. [...] | Eliminates tokens that might not contribute essential information to optimize the answer generation process of a reader. Reduces run-time by up to 62.2%, with only a 2% reduction in performance. | Optimizing Retrieval-augmented Reader Models via Token Elimination (opens in a new tab) | Oct 2023 |
| Instruction-tunes a small LM verifier to verify the output and the knowledge of the knowledge-augmented LMs with a separate verifier. It helps to address scenarios where the model may fail to retrieve the knowledge relevant to the given query, or where the model may not faithfully reflect the retrieved knowledge in the generated text. | Knowledge-Augmented Language Model Verification (opens in a new tab) | Oct 2023 | [...] | Adopts a small language model as a trainable rewriter to cater to a black-box LLM reader. The rewriter is trained using the feedback of the LLM reader by RL. Results in a new framework called Rewrite-Retrieve-Read where the focus is on optimizing queries. | Query Rewriting for Retrieval-Augmented Large Language Models (opens in a new tab) | May 2023 |
| Iteratively employs a retrieval-augmented generator to create an unbounded memory pool and uses a memory selector to choose one output as memory for the subsequent generation round. This enables a model to leverage its own output, referred to as self-memory, for improved generation. | Lift Yourself Up: Retrieval-augmented Text Generation with Self Memory (opens in a new tab) | May 2023 |
